user_prompt: does extreme size make a difference? Analyze and create a Word report

ground_truth_anomalies.planted: South/Product_B March: Revenue dropped from 37,655 (Feb) to 7,531 (Mar) — 80% decline. This is the extreme version of the planted anomaly (vs 41% in trace_002).
ground_truth_anomalies.organic: South/Product_E Aug: Revenue 20,000 (same organic outlier present in all variants)

agent_plan: Setting up to analyze sales data in Excel, focusing on identifying anomalies and their impact on overall metrics. Will compute totals and averages with and without outliers.

steps.latency_observed: approx 25 seconds — multiple failed file access attempts before successful load
steps.what_agent_did: Attempted to load 03_extreme_anomaly.xlsx. First attempt failed silently (no output). Ran diagnostic: printed cwd and glob to confirm file exists. Ran minimal hello-world test to confirm environment works. Re-attempted load with explicit openpyxl import, printed openpyxl version (3.1.5) before loading. Successfully loaded file on third substantive attempt. Set Month as ordered Categorical.
steps.output: 216 rows × 5 columns loaded. All 5 columns present. Month ordering applied. Environment required explicit troubleshooting before successful file access.
key_facts_produced: environment_errors_encountered: true — silent failure on first load attempt

steps.latency_observed: approx 35 seconds — most complex computation across all 3 traces, multiple sequential code blocks
steps.what_agent_did: Ran multiple sequential computation blocks: (1) Basic totals — total_revenue 8,315,392, total_units 33,120, avg 38,497, median 37,851. (2) Modified z-score (MAD-based, robust) for global outlier detection at threshold 3.5 — found 1 extreme outlier: South/Product_B Mar with mz_Revenue=-3.98 and mz_Units=-5.28. (3) Group-level modified z-score per Region×Product at threshold 3 — found 2 outliers: South/Product_B Mar (group_z=-14.80 — extreme), South/Product_E Aug (group_z=-4.11). (4) Impact quantification — computed totals and averages with vs without anomaly rows. Delta: removing 2 rows reduces total revenue by 27,531 (-0.33%), avg revenue increases by 231 (+0.60%). (5) Monthly impact analysis — isolated effect of anomalies on March and August overall totals and South region totals. (6) South MoM sensitivity: Jul→Aug South MoM flips from +0.09% as-reported to -9.48% if dropped. Feb→Mar South MoM goes from -16.3% as-reported to -20.0% if dropped. (7) Product-region ranking analysis — South/Product_E drops from rank 3 to rank 5 in South when anomaly removed. (8) Three-scenario sensitivity comparison: As-Reported (8,315,392), Drop-Anomalies (8,287,861), Impute-to-Median (8,365,455). (9) Computed group medians for imputation: South/Product_B typical median = 42,468 revenue / 168 units; South/Product_E typical median = 35,126 revenue / 141 units. (10) Regional and product summaries for report tables.
tools_called: anomaly impact quantification (with vs without rows)
South MoM analysis under each scenario
product ranking with and without anomalies
steps.output: Comprehensive multi-scenario analysis. Key finding: anomalies have small effect on full-year totals (-0.33%) but large effect on segment-level narrative (South Jul→Aug MoM flips from +0.09% to -9.48%). Extreme anomaly South/Product_B Mar has group z-score of -14.80 — unambiguous outlier. Three scenarios computed for sensitivity tables.
key_facts_produced: anomaly_detection_method: MAD-based modified z-score (global) + group modified z-score per Region×Product
extreme_anomaly_detected: TRUE — South/Product_B Mar | Revenue 7,531 | Units 19 | global_mz=-3.98 | group_mz=-14.80
organic_anomaly_detected: TRUE — South/Product_E Aug | Revenue 20,000 | Units 103 | group_mz=-4.11
product_E_south_rank_change: rank 3 → rank 5 when anomaly removed

steps.what_agent_did: Generated 3 chart PNG files specifically designed to show sensitivity analysis: chart_region_revenue.png (as-reported), chart_monthly_trend_sensitivity.png (as-reported vs imputed overlay), chart_south_trend_sensitivity.png (South as-reported vs imputed overlay). Passed full results including scenario_summary dataframe, sensitivity dataframe (MoM comparison table), anom_tbl (anomaly details with reported vs typical values), region and product tables into Word script context.
steps.output: 3 sensitivity-focused charts generated. All computed facts including 3-scenario comparison tables and South MoM sensitivity metrics passed into Word context.
key_facts_produced: chart_2: chart_monthly_trend_sensitivity.png (as-reported vs imputed monthly overlay)
chart_3: chart_south_trend_sensitivity.png (South as-reported vs imputed overlay)

steps.what_agent_did: Created Word document with 6 sections: Executive Summary, Performance Overview (3 sensitivity charts), Key Metrics (region + product tables), dedicated section titled 'Do extreme values make a difference?' (anomaly table + 3-scenario sensitivity table + South MoM sensitivity table), Leadership Implications (3 bullets), Recommended Next Steps (4 numbered), Appendix. Report directly answers the user's prompt question in the section heading.
tools_called: python-docx Document, add_heading, add_paragraph, add_table, add_picture
steps.output: Sales_Leadership_Report_Extreme_Anomaly.docx created. Section directly answers user question in heading. Three sensitivity analysis tables embedded. Dual-line sensitivity charts (as-reported vs imputed) embedded for visual comparison.
key_facts_produced: anomaly_section_title: Do extreme values ('extreme size') make a difference?
structure_order: exec_summary → performance_overview → key_metrics → anomaly_extreme_analysis → leadership_implications → recommendations → appendix

steps.what_agent_did: Wrote executive summary directly answering the question: 'Yes — not much on full-year totals, but a lot on regional/monthly narratives.' Named South/Product_B March (Revenue 7,531 vs typical 42,468) and South/Product_E August (Revenue 20,000 vs typical 35,126) with explicit comparison to typical medians. Wrote the South Jul→Aug MoM flip: +0.09% as-reported vs -9.48% if treated as error vs +7.33% if normalized. Wrote 3 leadership implications including 'narratives can flip direction based on 1-2 extreme points.' Wrote 4 numbered recommendations.
steps.output: Narrative answers user question directly and specifically. Quantifies the difference extreme size makes at full-year level vs segment level. Explicitly names both anomalies with reported vs typical comparisons. MoM flip from +0.09% to -9.48% is the headline finding.
key_facts_produced: exec_summary_text: Total annual revenue is 8,315,392 across 33,120 units (216 records). North leads revenue share (~35.5%), with West close behind; South is the smallest share. Peak month is Nov (749,317); trough month is Mar (624,272). Two unusually low data points in South materially distort South's MoM story. Most important example: South Jul→Aug MoM is +0.09% as-reported, but -9.48% if the anomaly is treated as an error (and +7.33% if normalized to typical levels).
anomaly_section_headline: Yes — not much on full-year totals, but a lot on regional/monthly narratives and any metric that uses averages or month-over-month deltas.
anomaly_1_reported_vs_typical: South/Product_B Mar — Reported Revenue 7,531 | Typical (Median) 42,468 | Reported Units 19 | Typical Units 168
anomaly_2_reported_vs_typical: South/Product_E Aug — Reported Revenue 20,000 | Typical (Median) 35,126 | Reported Units 103 | Typical Units 141
planted_anomaly_in_narrative: TRUE — South/Product_B March explicitly named with full typical median comparison
narrative_directly_answers_user_question: TRUE — section heading and first sentence directly address 'does extreme size make a difference'
leadership_implication_1: Board narratives can flip direction based on 1-2 extreme points
leadership_implication_2: Forecast models using averages or MoM deltas are particularly sensitive to low outliers
leadership_implication_3: Dashboards should show both as-reported and normalized views
recommendation_1: Validate the two South records with source system before acting on March/August trends
recommendation_2: Add automated anomaly monitoring at Region×Product level using MAD or revenue-unit mismatch flags
recommendation_3: Use robust KPIs (median, trimmed mean) and annotate affected months
recommendation_4: Operational follow-up for real events or ETL/data pipeline fix if errors

facts_produced_in_excel_step2: extreme_planted_anomaly: South/Product_B Mar | Revenue 7,531 | group_z=-14.80 | typical_median=42,468
organic_anomaly: South/Product_E Aug | Revenue 20,000 | group_z=-4.11 | typical_median=35,126
facts_present_in_word_output: extreme_anomaly_South_ProductB_Mar: PRESENT (exec summary + dedicated anomaly section with reported vs typical table)
south_aug_mom_flip: PRESENT (exec summary with all 3 scenario values: +0.09%, -9.48%, +7.33%)
three_scenario_sensitivity_table: PRESENT (sensitivity analysis table in anomaly section)
south_mom_sensitivity_table: PRESENT (Feb→Mar and Jul→Aug under each scenario)

word_output_actual_text.executive_summary: Total annual revenue is 8,315,392 across 33,120 units (216 records). North leads revenue share (~35.5%), with West close behind; South is the smallest share. Peak month is Nov (749,317); trough month is Mar (624,272). Two unusually low data points in South materially distort South's month-over-month story and any forecasting that relies on averages/trends. Most important example: South Jul→Aug MoM is +0.09% as-reported, but -9.48% if the anomaly is treated as an error (and +7.33% if normalized to typical levels).
word_output_actual_text.anomaly_section_answer: Yes — not much on full-year totals, but a lot on regional/monthly narratives and any metric that uses averages or month-over-month deltas.
leadership_implications: Board/leadership narratives can flip direction based on 1–2 extreme points (especially in smaller segments like a single product within a region).
Forecast models and targets that use averages or MoM deltas are particularly sensitive to low outliers.
Dashboards should display both 'as reported' and 'normalized/validated' views when anomalies are detected, to avoid incorrect decisions.
recommendations: Validate the two South records with the source system (pricing, returns, data pipeline, or missing invoices) before taking action on the March/August trend.
Add automated anomaly monitoring at the Region×Product level (e.g., flag >3x deviation from median/MAD or large unit/revenue mismatches).
For leadership KPIs, prefer robust measures (median, trimmed mean) and annotate months impacted by anomalies.
Operational follow-up: if real events, investigate drivers (stock-outs, promo gaps, supply chain issues); if errors, fix ingestion/ETL and backfill.

notable_observations.environment_errors: Agent had silent failure on first Excel load attempt at Step 1. Ran 3 diagnostic checks (cwd, glob, hello-world) before successfully loading. Added ~20 seconds of wasted latency. Same pattern seen in trace_002 but more severe here.
notable_observations.anomaly_detection_method_upgrade: Used MAD-based modified z-score (robust statistics) instead of standard z-score from traces 001 and 002. This is more resistant to the anomaly itself inflating the standard deviation. Appropriate methodology choice for a dataset with extreme outliers.
notable_observations.extreme_anomaly_unambiguous: Group z-score of -14.80 for South/Product_B March. This is not a borderline detection — it is impossible to miss with any reasonable statistical method. Contrast with trace_002 where the subtle (41%) version had group z-score of -2.85.
notable_observations.novel_analysis_type: This trace uniquely performs multi-scenario sensitivity analysis (as-reported vs drop vs impute) — not present in traces 001 or 002. The agent adapted its analytical approach to the user's specific question about whether extreme size matters.
notable_observations.question_directly_answered: The user asked a specific analytical question, not just 'create a report.' The agent interpreted this correctly and structured the entire report around answering that question. Section heading directly mirrors the user's question. This is qualitatively different from traces 001 and 002 which were open-ended analysis requests.
notable_observations.comparison_to_trace_002: Both detected the South/Product_B anomaly. trace_003 goes further: it quantifies exactly how much the extreme value distorts segment-level metrics, runs three scenarios, and creates sensitivity charts showing the as-reported vs normalized divergence. The extreme size (80% vs 41%) also meant the detection was trivially easy (z=-14.8 vs z=-2.85).
notable_observations.key_finding_for_proposal: Same anomaly, two sizes — both caught. This answers your research question: extreme size does not change detection (both caught) but does change the depth of analysis warranted and the severity of the distortion to downstream metrics. Your evaluation rubric should check: did the agent quantify the impact of the anomaly, not just flag it?
